UKIP would allow businesses to favour British workers over migrants,[[299]](#cite_note-wilfully_misinterpreted-299) and would repeal "much of" Britain's racial discrimination law, which was described as "shocking" by the [Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition government](/wiki/Conservative%E2%80%93Liberal_Democrat_coalition_government "Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition government")[[300]](#cite_note-300) and viewed as discriminatory by others.[[301]](#cite_note-301) However, Farage insisted that his comments regarding his party's policies on these matters had been "wilfully misinterpreted".[[299]](#cite_note-wilfully_misinterpreted-299) Although the party did not have an official stance on the [Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership](/wiki/Transatlantic_Trade_and_Investment_Partnership "Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership"), the party's former international trade spokesperson ([Lord Dartmouth](/wiki/William_Legge,_10th_Earl_of_Dartmouth "William Legge, 10th Earl of Dartmouth")) and former health and social care spokesperson ([Louise Bours](/wiki/Louise_Bours "Louise Bours")) stated that they did wish the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") to be included in the trade deal, according to the *[International Business Times](/wiki/International_Business_Times "International Business Times")*.[[302]](#cite_note-302)

### Social policy

In *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, commentator Ed Rooksby described UKIP's approach to many social issues as being "traditionalist and socially conservative",[[303]](#cite_note-303) while political scientist Stephen Driver has referred to the party's appeals to "traditional social values".[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDriver2011147-287) UKIP opposed the introduction of [same-sex marriage in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_Kingdom "Same-sex marriage in the United Kingdom").[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClements2014242Tournier-Sol2015146-304) UKIP wants to repeal the [Human Rights Act](/wiki/Human_Rights_Act_1998 "Human Rights Act 1998"),[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbediLundberg200973-305) and remove Britain from both the European Convention on Refugees and the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") (ECHR).[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEtheridge201442–43-306)[[307]](#cite_note-Immigration_policy-307) On the repeal of Britain's signatory to the ECHR, UKIP would like to see a referendum on the reintroduction of the [death penalty in the UK](/wiki/Death_penalty_in_the_UK "Death penalty in the UK").[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEtheridge201443-308)

In 2015, Farage attracted widespread press attention for suggesting that HIV positive patients who were not British citizens should not receive treatment on the NHS.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisonGoodwin2015168-309) In that same speech he stated that the UK should put the NHS "there for British people and families, who in many cases have paid into the system for years".[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisonGoodwin2015168-309) Farage has spoken in favour of an insurance-based system in the past, which he said would resemble the French and Dutch style system rather than an American style private system, but this was rejected by the party. He has commented, "we may have to think about ways in the future about dealing with health care differently".[[310]](#cite_note-310) Critics of UKIP have claimed that the party's real desire is to dismantle and [privatise](/wiki/Privatise "Privatise") the NHS,[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015134-311) a claim bolstered by the publication of leaked documents showing that in 2013 the UKIP NEC privately spoke positively of NHS privatisation.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015220-312)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Ukipppc2010.jpg/250px-Ukipppc2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukipppc2010.jpg)

A UKIP candidate campaigning in the run-up to the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election")

Although Farage had long been reticent about focusing on public anxieties surrounding Muslims in Britain, he spoke out following the [*Charlie Hebdo* shooting](/wiki/Charlie_Hebdo_shooting "Charlie Hebdo shooting"), claiming that there was a "fifth column" of Islamists in the UK who—while "mercifully small" in number—were "out to destroy our whole civilisation".[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015214-313) At the same time he called for Western states to do more to promote their [Judeo-Christian](/wiki/Judeo-Christian "Judeo-Christian") heritage,[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015214-313) and criticised state [multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") for promoting social segregation, discouraging integration, and generating a "tick-box approach" to [identity politics](/wiki/Identity_politics "Identity politics").[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015214-313) In its 2017 manifesto, UKIP pledged to abolish the existence of [sharia courts](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") in the UK and ban the wearing of the [niqab](/wiki/Niqab "Niqab") and burka in public; it claimed that these were needed to promote the integration of Muslims with wider British society.[[314]](#cite_note-314)

UKIP is the only major political party in the United Kingdom that does not endorse [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") and lower carbon emissions,[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReed2016229-315) and its media output regularly promotes [climate change denial](/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial").[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReed2016237-316) Farage and other senior UKIP figures have repeatedly spoken out against the construction of [wind farms](/wiki/Wind_farms "Wind farms"), deeming them a blot on the rural landscape.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReed2016233–36-317) UKIP's media present renewable energy as inefficient and unaffordable,[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReed2016237-316) and they promote the use of [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels"), [nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") and [fracking](/wiki/Fracking "Fracking").[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReed2016238-318) UKIP has announced that it would repeal the [Climate Change Act 2008](/wiki/Climate_Change_Act_2008 "Climate Change Act 2008") and has placed an emphasis on protecting the [Green Belt](/wiki/Green_belt_(United_Kingdom) "Green belt (United Kingdom)").[[319]](#cite_note-319)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/UKIP_poster_in_Alexandra_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1362476.jpg/250px-UKIP_poster_in_Alexandra_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1362476.jpg)](/wiki/File:UKIP_poster_in_Alexandra_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1362476.jpg)

UKIP poster in [Egham](/wiki/Egham "Egham"), Surrey, for the 2009 European elections

In its 2015 election manifesto, UKIP promised to teach a chronological understanding of "British history and achievements" in schools,[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayton2016403-255) and it calls for the scrapping of [sex education](/wiki/Sex_education "Sex education") for children under 11.[[320]](#cite_note-Education-320) UKIP would introduce an option for students to take an apprenticeship qualification instead of four non-core [GCSEs](/wiki/General_Certificate_of_Secondary_Education "General Certificate of Secondary Education") which can be continued at [A Level](/wiki/GCE_Advanced_Level "GCE Advanced Level").[[320]](#cite_note-Education-320) Schools would be investigated by [OFSTED](/wiki/OFSTED "OFSTED") on the presentation of a petition to the Department for Education signed by 25% of parents or governors.[[320]](#cite_note-Education-320) UKIP have promoted the scrapping of the government target that 50% of school leavers attend university, and present the policy that [tuition fees](/wiki/Tuition_fees "Tuition fees") would be scrapped for students taking approved degrees in science, medicine, technology, engineering or mathematics.[[320]](#cite_note-Education-320)

Farage argued that [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") like [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") should have representatives in the [House of Commons of the United Kingdom](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), akin to the privileges given to French overseas territories in France. Farage believes that all citizens for whom the British Parliament passes legislation, whether in the United Kingdom or its territories, deserve democratic representation in that Parliament.[[321]](#cite_note-321)

## Support

### Financial backing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/UKIP_campaigning_in_Newport_High_Street.jpg/250px-UKIP_campaigning_in_Newport_High_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:UKIP_campaigning_in_Newport_High_Street.jpg)

[Tadley Town](/wiki/Tadley "Tadley") councillor and UKIP candidate for the [Hampshire Police and Crime Commissioner](/wiki/Hampshire_Police_and_Crime_Commissioner "Hampshire Police and Crime Commissioner"), Stephen West, campaigning in [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Isle_of_Wight "Newport, Isle of Wight") high street on the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight") in [2012](/wiki/2012_England_and_Wales_police_and_crime_commissioner_elections "2012 England and Wales police and crime commissioner elections").

In 2008, Usherwood noted that UKIP relied heavily on a small number of major financial backers.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUsherwood2008261-322) According to *The Guardian*, a leaked internal report to UKIP's executive committee dated to September 2012 shows that the party's leader argued that "the key to money for us will be the [hedge fund](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund") industry".[[323]](#cite_note-323)

According to UKIP's annual returns to the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)"),[[324]](#cite_note-324) in 2013 the party had a total income of £2,479,314. Of this, £714,492 was from membership and subscriptions, £32,115 from fundraising activities and £1,361,640 from donations. By law, individual donations over £7,500 must be reported.[[325]](#cite_note-325)

UKIP has several high-profile backers. In March 2009, the Conservative Party's biggest-ever donor, Stuart Wheeler, donated £100,000 to UKIP after criticising Cameron's stance towards the Treaty of Lisbon. He was then expelled from the Conservatives and in 2011 appointed treasurer of UKIP.[[326]](#cite_note-326) In October 2014, [Arron Banks](/wiki/Arron_Banks "Arron Banks"), who previously gave £25,000 to the Conservatives, increased his UKIP donation from £100,000 to £1 million after Hague said he had never heard of him.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015151–152-327)[[328]](#cite_note-Halliday-328) The multi-millionaire [Paul Sykes](/wiki/Paul_Sykes_(businessman) "Paul Sykes (businessman)") has helped finance the party, donating over £1 million to its 2014 campaign at the European Parliament.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201537-329)

In December 2014, [Richard Desmond](/wiki/Richard_Desmond "Richard Desmond"), proprietor of [Express Newspapers](/wiki/Express_Newspapers "Express Newspapers"), donated £300,000 to UKIP.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016178-87) Desmond had previously made the UKIP peer [David Stevens](/wiki/David_Stevens,_Baron_Stevens_of_Ludgate "David Stevens, Baron Stevens of Ludgate") his deputy chairman.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016183-274)[[328]](#cite_note-Halliday-328) The donation indicated that Desmond's papers, the *[Daily Express](/wiki/Daily_Express "Daily Express")*, *[Sunday Express](/wiki/Sunday_Express "Sunday Express")*, *[Daily Star](/wiki/Daily_Star_(British_newspaper) "Daily Star (British newspaper)")* and *[Daily Star Sunday](/wiki/Daily_Star_Sunday "Daily Star Sunday")*, would back UKIP in the 2015 general election.[[330]](#cite_note-330) Three weeks before the election, Desmond gave the party a further £1 million.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016178-87)[[331]](#cite_note-331)

In September 2016, the major UKIP donor, Arron Banks, said that UKIP would be "dead in the water" if [Diane James](/wiki/Diane_James "Diane James") did not become leader.[[332]](#cite_note-332) Following her departure after 18 days, Banks said that he would leave UKIP if [Steven Woolfe](/wiki/Steven_Woolfe "Steven Woolfe") was prevented from running for leader, and if two other members remained in the party: "If [Neil Hamilton](/wiki/Neil_Hamilton_(politician) "Neil Hamilton (politician)") and [Douglas Carswell](/wiki/Douglas_Carswell "Douglas Carswell") [UKIP's only MP] remain in the party, and the NEC decide that Steven Woolfe cannot run for leader, I will be leaving UKIP".[[333]](#cite_note-333)

### Membership

UKIP's membership numbers increased from 2002 to the time of the 2004 European Parliament election, before hovering around the 16,000 mark during the late 2000s.[[30]](#cite_note-BBC3May13-30)[[334]](#cite_note-brief-334) In 2004, the party claimed 20,000 members, with this remaining broadly stable, and in June 2007 it had a recorded 16,700 members.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbediLundberg200980-335) By July 2013, the figure had grown to 30,000[[336]](#cite_note-336) before ending the year at 32,447.[[337]](#cite_note-337) In 2014, the number was 36,000 on 22 April,[[338]](#cite_note-ExpressApril22-338) by 7 May reached 37,000[[339]](#cite_note-ExpressMay7-339) and on 19 May, less than a fortnight later and only three days before the 2014 European Parliament election, rose to 38,000.[[340]](#cite_note-340) In January 2015, UKIP membership was the fifth-highest of British parties.[[341]](#cite_note-BBC_News_15_January_2015-341)[[342]](#cite_note-342)

Membership was 45,000 in May 2015, but since then has fallen to 32,757 in November 2016,[[343]](#cite_note-343) and as low as 18,000 under Henry Bolton by January 2018.[[344]](#cite_note-344)

In June 2018, four political activists known through social media – [Paul Joseph Watson](/wiki/Paul_Joseph_Watson "Paul Joseph Watson"), [Mark Meechan](/wiki/Mark_Meechan "Mark Meechan"), [Carl Benjamin](/wiki/Carl_Benjamin "Carl Benjamin"), and [Milo Yiannopoulos](/wiki/Milo_Yiannopoulos "Milo Yiannopoulos") – joined the party. This was followed by the party gaining around 500 members.[[345]](#cite_note-345)

In July 2018, it was reported the party had attracted 3,200 new members, a 15% increase.[[346]](#cite_note-346)

The party's report to The Electoral Commission of its accounts as of 31 December 2020 stated the party had a membership of 3,888.[[5]](#cite_note-31_December_2020_membership-5)

### Voter base

> UKIP's voters are not single-issue Europhobes or political protesters, they share a clear and distinct agenda, mixing deep Euroscepticism with clear ideas about immigration, national identity and the way British society is changing. The conflict between UKIP's voters and the political mainstream reflects a deep-seated difference in outlook among voters from different walks in life. Those who lead and staff the three main parties are all from the highly educated, socially liberal middle classes, who are comfortable in an ethnically and culturally diverse, outward looking society... Those who lead and staff UKIP, and those who vote for them, are older, less educated, disadvantaged and economically insecure Britons, who are profoundly uncomfortable in the 'new' society, which they regard as alien and threatening.

— Political scientists Robert Ford and Matthew Goodwin, 2014.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin201411-347)

In its early years, UKIP targeted itself towards southern English, middle-class Eurosceptic voters, those who had been supporters of the Conservative Party until [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major")'s Conservative government signed the Maastricht Treaty.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014108-348) This led to the widespread perception that UKIP's supporters were primarily middle-class ex-Conservative voters, with commentator [Peter Oborne](/wiki/Peter_Oborne "Peter Oborne") characterising UKIP as "the Conservative Party in exile".[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014145-349)

After 2009, UKIP refocused its attention to appeal primarily to [white British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), working-class, blue-collar workers; those who had traditionally voted Labour or in some cases for Thatcher's Conservatives but who had ceased voting or begun to vote BNP since the emergence of the [New Labour](/wiki/New_Labour "New Labour") project in the 1990s.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014108-348) In this way, UKIP's support base does not line up with the historical left-right divide in British politics, instead being primarily rooted in class divisions.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014270-350) This mirrored the voting base of other radical right parties across Western Europe which had grown since the early 1990s.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014111–112-351) This scenario had come about following the rapid growth of the middle-classes and the concomitant decline of the working-class population in Western Europe; the centre-left, social-democratic parties who had traditionally courted the support of the working classes largely switched their attention to the newly emergent middle-classes, leaving their initial support base increasingly alienated and creating the vacuum which the radical right exploited.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014113–114-352)

On the basis of their extensive study of data on the subject, in 2014 the political scientists [Matthew Goodwin](/wiki/Matt_Goodwin_(author) "Matt Goodwin (author)") and [Robert Ford](/wiki/Robert_Ford_(academic) "Robert Ford (academic)") concluded that "UKIP's support has a very clear social profile, more so than any of the mainstream parties. Their electoral base is old, male, working class, white and less educated".[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014159-353) They found that 57% of professed UKIP supporters were over the age of 54, while only one in 10 was under 35, which they attributed to the fact that UKIP's socially conservative and Eurosceptic platform appealed far more to Britain's older generations that their younger counterparts, who were more socially liberal and less antagonistic towards the EU.[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014156–158-354)

Males accounted for 57% of UKIP supporters, which Ford and Goodwin suggested was due to women voters being put off by a number of high-profile [sexist](/wiki/Sexist "Sexist") remarks made by UKIP candidates.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014154–155-355) A vast majority of UKIP supporters, 99.6%, identified as white, reflecting the fact that ethnic minorities tended to avoid the party.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014158–159-356) Over half, or 55%, of UKIP supporters had left school aged 16 or under, with only 24% having attended university, suggesting that the party primarily appealed to the least educated voters in society.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014154-357) Ford and Goodwin also found that UKIP's support base was more working-class than that of any other party, with 42% of supporters in [blue-collar](/wiki/Blue-collar "Blue-collar") jobs.[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014152-358) Ford and Goodwin described UKIP's voters as primarily comprising the "left behind" sector of society, "older, less skilled and less well educated working-class voters" who felt disenfranchised from the mainstream political parties which had increasingly focused on attracting the support of middle-class [swing voters](/wiki/Swing_voters "Swing voters").[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin201410-359)

Ford and Goodwin nevertheless noted that UKIP was "not a purely blue-collar party but an alliance of manual workers, employers and the self-employed."[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2016482-360)
Geoffrey Evans and Jon Mellon highlighted that UKIP receive "a greater proportion of their support from lower professionals and managers" than from any other class group.[[361]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvansMellon2016474-361) They highlighted that polls repeatedly demonstrated that UKIP drew more votes from Conservative voters than Labour ones.[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvansMellon2016464–465-362) They suggested that the assumption that working-class voters who supported UKIP had previously been Labour voters was misplaced,[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvansMellon2016465-363) suggesting that these people had ceased voting for Labour "a long time before UKIP were an effective political presence", having been alienated by Labour's "pro-middle class, pro-EU and, as it eventually turned out, pro-immigration agenda".[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvansMellon2016467-364)
In 2011, Goodwin, Ford, and David Cutts published a study that identified Euroscepticism as the main causal factor for voters supporting UKIP, with concern over immigration levels and distrust of the political establishment also featuring as important motives.[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwinCutts2011204-365) They noted, however, that during elections for the European Parliament, UKIP was able to broaden its support to gain the vote of largely middle-class Eurosceptics who vote Conservative in other elections.[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014231-366)

> Ukip has become more than the single issue on which it was founded: under Farage's leadership it has become a welcoming home for the many in British society who feel that 'the system' isn't working for them, or has left them behind, economically, socially or politically. In so doing, it has gained supporters from across the political spectrum, including many old Labour voters in economically distressed regions of the country.

— Political scientist Simon Usherwood, 2016.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUsherwood2016a29-114)

From their analysis of the data, Ford and Goodwin stated that UKIP's support base has "strong parallels" both with that of western Europe's other radical right parties and with the BNP during their electoral heyday.[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014273-367) Conversely, an earlier study by Richard Whitaker and Philip Lynch, based on polling data from [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov"), concluded that UKIP voters were distinct from those of far-right parties. The authors found that voter support for UKIP correlated with concerns about the value of immigration and a lack of trust in the political system, but the biggest explanatory factor for their support of UKIP was Euroscepticism.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitakerLynch2011359-368) A further study by the same authors suggests that UKIP voters' core beliefs align very closely to those of the UKIP candidates; particularly so on issues surrounding European integration, which has resulted in Conservative voters switching to UKIP due to Conservative divisions on this issue.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynchWhitakerLoomes2012-369) One study found that 63% of UKIP voters considered themselves to be right-wing, while 22% thought centrist and 16% thought leftist.[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201592-370) 81% believed that immigration undermined British culture, a view shared by only half the wider British population.[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201589–90-371) On economic issues, there was a divide between UKIP voters and the party itself.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201593-372) In contrast to the party's economic liberalism, UKIP supporters often held more leftist attitudes to the economy, with almost 80% opining that [big business](/wiki/Big_business "Big business") took advantage of working people and almost 70% thinking that privatisation had gone too far.[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201594-373)

UKIP has been most successful along England's eastern and southern coasts, in parts of south-west England, and in the Labour heartlands of Northern England and Wales.[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201573-374) It has not done well in London and in [university towns](/wiki/University_town "University town") and urban areas with younger populations like Oxford, Cambridge, Manchester, and Brighton.[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201573,_74–75-375) It has done well in areas with large numbers of old, white, and poorer people, and weaker in areas with larger numbers of younger, more ethnically and culturally diverse, and financially secure people.[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201573-374)
Ford and Goodwin noted that UKIP "barely registers" with young Britons, graduates, ethnic minorities, and [pro-EU](/wiki/Pro-EU "Pro-EU") voters.[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014256-376)
According to an Opinium poll in December 2014 on the views of 17- to 22-year-olds, Farage was the least popular political leader. Only 3% of young people questioned said that they intended to vote for UKIP, compared with 19% among voters of all ages.[[377]](#cite_note-377) The 17% who said they would vote outside the three main parties were four times more likely to vote for the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") than for UKIP.[[378]](#cite_note-378) Conversely, a March 2015 [Ipsos Mori](/wiki/Ipsos_Mori "Ipsos Mori") poll found among 18- to 34-year-olds UKIP was polling nearly as well as the Green Party, somewhat contradicting the idea that Farage lacked appeal for younger voters.[[379]](#cite_note-379) On the basis of their fieldwork among supporters of the [English Defence League](/wiki/English_Defence_League "English Defence League") (EDL), an anti-Islam social movement, Winlow, Hall, and Treadwell noted that most EDL supporters whom they encountered intended to vote for UKIP in the build-up to the 2015 general election.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinlowHallTreadwell201776-380)

UKIP supporters are sometimes nicknamed "Kippers". In May 2017, in response to large defections from the party, Goodwin said "Former Kippers did not walk but literally sprinted over to the Conservatives."[[381]](#cite_note-381)

According to Part VII of the UKIP constitution, the party leader is voted for by postal ballot by all paid-up party members "in good standing". The winner is the candidate with the [simple majority](/wiki/Majority "Majority") of votes cast. If there is only one valid candidate, they are elected without a ballot. While the default term is four years, the leader can extend up to a year if there is an imminent General or European Parliament election; this must be approved by at least two-thirds of the 12-person NEC. If at least nine NEC members endorse a [vote of no confidence](/wiki/Vote_of_no_confidence "Vote of no confidence") in the leader, an emergency general meeting (EGM) will be called. When the leadership becomes vacant unexpectedly, the NEC has 14 days to name an interim leader who exercises all leadership functions until the next leadership election. The leader has the power to name a Deputy Leader of their own choice and assign them any duty they choose.[[382]](#cite_note-const-382)

### Regions

UKIP's organisation is divided into 12 regions: London, South East, South West, Eastern, East Midlands, West Midlands, Yorkshire, North East, North West, Wales, Northern Ireland, and Scotland.[[387]](#cite_note-388) An additional 13th branch operates in the [British Overseas Territory](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") of [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"); it held its first public meeting at the Lord Nelson pub in April 2013.[[388]](#cite_note-389)

At the end of 2013, UKIP Scotland was dissolved after infighting tore the regional party apart; the party's administrative body was dissolved, Mike Scott-Hayward (the chairman and chief fundraiser) quit, and Farage fired Lord [(Christopher) Monckton](/wiki/Christopher_Monckton "Christopher Monckton") via email.[[389]](#cite_note-wipeout-390) The national party and UKIP Scotland focused on supporting the candidates for the [2014 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom](/wiki/2014_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2014 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom").[[389]](#cite_note-wipeout-390) After [David Coburn](/wiki/David_Coburn_(politician) "David Coburn (politician)") won an MEP seat in Scotland in those elections, he was elected as leader of UKIP Scotland.[[390]](#cite_note-391)

Veteran and former long-serving Antrim and Newtownabbey-based councillor, Robert Hill was appointed by Gerard Batten as UKIP's Spokesman for Northern Ireland in May 2018.[[391]](#cite_note-392) In August 2018, Welsh Assembly Member [Gareth Bennett](/wiki/Gareth_Bennett_(politician) "Gareth Bennett (politician)") was elected as leader of UKIP in Wales after a membership ballot.[[392]](#cite_note-393)

## Representatives

Main article: [UK Independence Party representation and election results](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party_representation_and_election_results "UK Independence Party representation and election results")

### House of Commons

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Douglas_Carswell.jpg/250px-Douglas_Carswell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Douglas_Carswell.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Mark_Reckless_AM_%2827889492560%29.jpg/250px-Mark_Reckless_AM_%2827889492560%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_Reckless_AM_(27889492560).jpg)

[Douglas Carswell](/wiki/Douglas_Carswell "Douglas Carswell") and [Mark Reckless](/wiki/Mark_Reckless "Mark Reckless"), UKIP's only elected MPs. The former represented UKIP from 2014 to 2017; the latter from 2014 to 2015.

In the UK, the [first-past-the-post voting](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") system for electing MPs to the House of Commons was a significant barrier to UKIP, whose support was widely distributed across different areas rather than being strongly focused in particular constituencies.[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014220–21-394) Further, the system encouraged [tactical voting](/wiki/Tactical_voting "Tactical voting"), with many UKIP supporters believing that a vote for the party would be a [wasted vote](/wiki/Wasted_vote "Wasted vote").[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014221-395) Recognising this, Farage believed that the best way to win a seat in the House of Commons was to win a [by-election](/wiki/By-election "By-election"), with UKIP contesting a number of these from 2010 onward.[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014241–42-396) Over the next few years, it contested a number of by-elections around the country, coming second in both [Barnsley Central](/wiki/2011_Barnsley_Central_by-election "2011 Barnsley Central by-election") and [Rotherham](/wiki/2012_Rotherham_by-election "2012 Rotherham by-election").[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014243,_246GoodwinMilazzo201536-397)
In 2008, [Bob Spink](/wiki/Bob_Spink "Bob Spink"), the MP for [Castle Point](/wiki/Castle_Point_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Castle Point (UK Parliament constituency)"), resigned the Tory [whip](/wiki/Whip_(politics) "Whip (politics)") (becoming an [Independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician")), but in April that year joined UKIP.[[397]](#cite_note-398) However, in November he appeared again as an Independent in Commons proceedings,[[398]](#cite_note-399) ultimately losing the seat to a Conservative in [2010](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election").

In 2014, two Conservative MPs changed allegiance to UKIP and resigned their seats to fight by-elections for UKIP. [Douglas Carswell](/wiki/Douglas_Carswell "Douglas Carswell") won the [Clacton by-election](/wiki/2014_Clacton_by-election "2014 Clacton by-election") on 9 October, making him the first MP to be elected representing UKIP.[[399]](#cite_note-400) [Mark Reckless](/wiki/Mark_Reckless "Mark Reckless") was also victorious in the Rochester and Strood by-election on 20 November.[[98]](#cite_note-reckless-98)
At the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), Carswell kept his seat in Clacton but Reckless lost Rochester to the Conservative [Kelly Tolhurst](/wiki/Kelly_Tolhurst "Kelly Tolhurst").[[400]](#cite_note-401) UKIP had 3,881,129 votes (12.6%) and was the third largest party on vote share, yet it won only one seat.[[401]](#cite_note-402) Because of this, there were calls from some in UKIP for a voting reform in favour of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").[[402]](#cite_note-403) Carswell quit the party in March 2017 to become an independent, leaving UKIP without any MPs in the Commons.[[118]](#cite_note-bbc3-118)
In the [2017 election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), a [snap election](/wiki/Snap_election "Snap election") initiated by PM [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") and scheduled for 8 June 2017, UKIP got 1.9% of the votes (after 12.6% in the [2015 election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election")) and no seats in the House of Commons.

### House of Lords

On 24 June 1995, UKIP gained its first member of the House of Lords, [Lord Grantley](/wiki/Richard_Norton,_8th_Baron_Grantley "Richard Norton, 8th Baron Grantley"), who had joined the party in 1993 from the Conservatives and had recently succeeded to his father's titles. However, with the coming [House of Lords Act 1999](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Act_1999 "House of Lords Act 1999"), he decided not to stand for election as a continuing member, and so left the House in November 1999. Earlier in 1999, UKIP had gained a second peer in the House of Lords, [The Earl of Bradford](/wiki/Richard_Thomas_Orlando_Bridgeman,_7th_Earl_of_Bradford "Richard Thomas Orlando Bridgeman, 7th Earl of Bradford"), but he, too, left the House in November 1999 because of the House of Lords Act. [Lord Pearson of Rannoch](/wiki/Malcolm_Pearson,_Baron_Pearson_of_Rannoch "Malcolm Pearson, Baron Pearson of Rannoch") and [Lord Willoughby de Broke](/wiki/David_Verney,_21st_Baron_Willoughby_de_Broke "David Verney, 21st Baron Willoughby de Broke") both defected to UKIP in 2007,[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynchWhitakerLoomes2012736-404) giving the party its first representation in the House of Lords since the departure of Lord Grantley and Lord Bradford.[[404]](#cite_note-405) Lord Pearson went on to serve as party leader from November 2009 to September 2010. On 18 September 2012, [Lord Stevens of Ludgate](/wiki/David_Stevens,_Baron_Stevens_of_Ludgate "David Stevens, Baron Stevens of Ludgate") joined UKIP, having sat as an Independent Conservative since his expulsion from the Conservatives in 2004.[[405]](#cite_note-406) In Autumn 2018, Lord Willoughby de Broke left UKIP, reducing the party's representation in the upper house back down to two.[[406]](#cite_note-407) Lord Stevens also left the party, in December 2018, leaving former leader Lord Pearson as UKIP's sole peer.[[407]](#cite_note-408) In October 2019, Lord Pearson resigned his membership of the party.

### Devolved parliaments and assemblies

UKIP competes electorally in all four parts of the United Kingdom.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayton2016401-232)

#### Northern Ireland

In October 2012, UKIP gained its first representation in a devolved Assembly the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") in [David McNarry](/wiki/David_McNarry "David McNarry"), [MLA](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly_(Northern_Ireland) "Member of the Legislative Assembly (Northern Ireland)") for [Strangford](/wiki/Strangford_(Assembly_constituency) "Strangford (Assembly constituency)"), who had left the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party").[[408]](#cite_note-409) The party however failed to continue its representation at the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_Northern_Ireland_Assembly_election "2016 Northern Ireland Assembly election"), coming within a hundred votes of taking a seat in [East Antrim](/wiki/East_Antrim_(Assembly_constituency) "East Antrim (Assembly constituency)").[[409]](#cite_note-newsletter.co.uk-410) This seat was unsuccessfully contested in the [2017 election](/wiki/2017_Northern_Ireland_Assembly_election "2017 Northern Ireland Assembly election").

#### Scotland

UKIP's support has been particularly weak in Scotland, where it has no representatives in the devolved parliament.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014244-411) UKIP fielded candidates at the [Scottish Parliament election on 5 May 2011](/wiki/2011_Scottish_Parliament_election "2011 Scottish Parliament election"), when its platform included a commitment to keep the Scottish Parliament in Edinburgh, while replacing the separately-elected [Members of the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament") with the [Members of the House of Commons elected in Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Grand_Committee "Scottish Grand Committee").[[411]](#cite_note-412)
The party fielded candidates on the regional lists in the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_Scottish_Parliament_election "2016 Scottish Parliament election") without any success.[[412]](#cite_note-413) In the [2021 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2021_Scottish_Parliament_election "2021 Scottish Parliament election") candidates were again fielded on regional lists.[[413]](#cite_note-414)

#### Wales

The party also fielded candidates for the [Senedd](/wiki/National_Assembly_for_Wales "National Assembly for Wales").[[414]](#cite_note-415) In the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_National_Assembly_for_Wales_election "2016 National Assembly for Wales election"), it entered the Assembly for the first time, winning seven of 60 seats.[[415]](#cite_note-416) However, following the resignations of [Caroline Jones](/wiki/Caroline_Jones_(politician) "Caroline Jones (politician)"), [Mark Reckless](/wiki/Mark_Reckless "Mark Reckless"), [Nathan Gill](/wiki/Nathan_Gill "Nathan Gill") and [Michelle Brown](/wiki/Michelle_Brown_(politician) "Michelle Brown (politician)"), by March 2019 the party's representation had fallen to three AMs.[[416]](#cite_note-Welsh3-417) UKIP ceased to have a formal Welsh Assembly group after [David Rowlands](/wiki/David_Rowlands_(Welsh_politician) "David Rowlands (Welsh politician)") resigned in May 2019 to form a new Brexit Party group with Reckless, Jones and [Mandy Jones](/wiki/Mandy_Jones_(politician) "Mandy Jones (politician)") (who had replaced Nathan Gill on his resignation as an AM).[[417]](#cite_note-Welsh2-418)
UKIP were left without any Senedd members after the [2021 Senedd election](/wiki/2021_Senedd_election "2021 Senedd election")[[418]](#cite_note-419)

### Local government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/UK_Independence_Party_offices_in_Tunbridge_Wells_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1731970.jpg/250px-UK_Independence_Party_offices_in_Tunbridge_Wells_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1731970.jpg)](/wiki/File:UK_Independence_Party_offices_in_Tunbridge_Wells_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1731970.jpg)

UKIP office in [Tunbridge Wells](/wiki/Royal_Tunbridge_Wells "Royal Tunbridge Wells")

UKIP initially paid little attention to local government elections. However, this changed after Farage observed that building localised strongholds of support in various parts of the country had been the process by which the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") had entered the House of Commons, and that this was a strategy that could benefit UKIP.[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014250-420) UKIP subsequently focused on the [2011 local elections](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2011 United Kingdom local elections"), in which it fielded over 1,100 candidates, winning seven seats and becoming the main opposition in over 100.[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014250–51-421)

The first UKIP local council election win occurred when one of its members was elected to [South Cambridgeshire District Council](/wiki/South_Cambridgeshire_District_Council "South Cambridgeshire District Council") in 2000. A number of Conservative, Liberal Democrat, Labour and Independent local councillors in all four constituent nations of the UK defected to UKIP over subsequent years, with the most recent defections to date (May to July 2013) coming from former Conservative councillors in the London Boroughs of [Merton](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Merton "London Borough of Merton"), [Richmond upon Thames](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Richmond_upon_Thames "London Borough of Richmond upon Thames") and [Havering](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Havering "London Borough of Havering"), and from Labour in [Northampton](/wiki/Northampton "Northampton") and [North-East Lincolnshire](/wiki/North-East_Lincolnshire "North-East Lincolnshire"). In May 2013, 33 English and one Welsh council held local elections, with UKIP gaining 139 seats for a total of 147, with significant gains in [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire_County_Council "Lincolnshire County Council"), [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk_County_Council "Norfolk County Council") and [Kent](/wiki/Kent_County_Council "Kent County Council").[[421]](#cite_note-422)

In the 2013 local elections, UKIP won 147 seats and established itself as the largest opposition party in six English county councils.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014253-423) At the [2013](/wiki/2013_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2013 United Kingdom local elections") and [2014](/wiki/2014_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2014 United Kingdom local elections") local elections, UKIP made significant gains to become the fourth largest party in terms of councillors in England, and fifth largest in the UK, with over 300 seats (out of about 21,000). In the [2015 local elections](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2015 United Kingdom local elections"), UKIP took control of [Thanet District Council](/wiki/Thanet_District_Council "Thanet District Council"), its first majority control of a council.[[423]](#cite_note-Council-424) However, the party lost control later in the year after several of its councillors defected and it lost its majority. UKIP later took back control as a majority after winning the 2016 Northwood ward by-election, taking its number of councillors up to 29. In the [2016 local elections](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2016 United Kingdom local elections"), UKIP won 58 council seats, an increase of 25.[[424]](#cite_note-425) In the [2017 United Kingdom local elections](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2017 United Kingdom local elections"), UKIP lost all of the seats it was defending but gained one from Labour on [Lancashire County Council](/wiki/Lancashire_County_Council "Lancashire County Council").[[120]](#cite_note-BBC050517-120) in the [2018 United Kingdom local elections](/wiki/2018_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2018 United Kingdom local elections"), UKIP lost all but three of the 126 seats it was defending.[[425]](#cite_note-426) In the
[2019 United Kingdom local elections](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2019 United Kingdom local elections"), UKIP suffered severe losses, with its number of councillors collapsing by 145 to 31, in the districts where votes were held that year. Its worst result was in Thanet where it lost 33 councillors.[[426]](#cite_note-BBC050519-427)

In the [2021 United Kingdom local elections](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2021 United Kingdom local elections") the party's support fell away and all the 48 council seats in England the party were defending were lost. No seats were won in the [London Assembly](/wiki/London_Assembly "London Assembly"), [Police and Crime Commissioners](/wiki/Police_and_Crime_Commissioners "Police and Crime Commissioners") or elections for mayors.[[427]](#cite_note-428) The [2022 local elections](/wiki/2022_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2022 United Kingdom local elections") saw the party lose all three seats it was defending from 2018.[[428]](#cite_note-429)

In the [2023 local elections](/wiki/2023_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2023 United Kingdom local elections"), the party lost all remaining representation at council level; only one incumbent councillor defended their seat,[[429]](#cite_note-BBC2023-430) out of the six seats UKIP contested,[[430]](#cite_note-431) and losing 25 councillors from 2019, the last time the seats were up for election.[[431]](#cite_note-432) The party now only has elected representatives on parish and town councils, the lowest level of local government; party chairman Ben Walker said the party had "about 30 parish councillors".[[429]](#cite_note-BBC2023-430) During the elections, Labour made significant inroads into Leave-voting areas that UKIP in which were formerly strongly competitive,[[432]](#cite_note-433) and notably won overall control of [Thanet District Council](/wiki/Thanet_District_Council_elections "Thanet District Council elections"),[[433]](#cite_note-434) that UKIP either controlled or were the largest party on between 2015 and 2019.

After a period without elected representation, UKIP saw a surprise defection in September 2025, in the form of Amelia Randall, a [Kent County Councillor](/wiki/Kent_County_Council "Kent County Council") for the [Birchington](/wiki/Birchington "Birchington") and Rural ward, who joined the party.[[434]](#cite_note-435)[[435]](#cite_note-436)
She had been elected as a Reform UK candidate in the [May election](/wiki/2025_Kent_County_Council_election "2025 Kent County Council election"), and had stood for the party in [Herne Bay and Sandwich](/wiki/Herne_Bay_and_Sandwich_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Herne Bay and Sandwich (UK Parliament constituency)") at the 2024 general election.[[436]](#cite_note-437)[[437]](#cite_note-438)[[438]](#cite_note-439)[[439]](#cite_note-440)

### European Parliament

As a result of its hard Eurosceptic approach, UKIP does not recognise the legitimacy of the European Parliament, and under Sked's leadership refused to take any of the EP seats that it won.[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUsherwood2008257-441) This changed after 1997, when the party decided that its elected representatives would take such seats to publicise its anti-EU agenda.[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUsherwood2008257-441)
As a result of the [1999 European parliament election](/wiki/1999_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "1999 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), three UKIP MEPs were elected to the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"). Together with Eurosceptic parties from other nations, they formed a new [European parliamentary group](/wiki/Political_groups_of_the_European_Parliament "Political groups of the European Parliament") called [Europe of Democracies and Diversities](/wiki/Europe_of_Democracies_and_Diversities "Europe of Democracies and Diversities") (EDD).[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhinnemoreMcGowan2013276-442)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Nicolas_Dupont_Aignan_et_Nigel_Farage.jpg/250px-Nicolas_Dupont_Aignan_et_Nigel_Farage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicolas_Dupont_Aignan_et_Nigel_Farage.jpg)

Farage with [France Arise](/wiki/France_Arise "France Arise") leader [Nicolas Dupont-Aignan](/wiki/Nicolas_Dupont-Aignan "Nicolas Dupont-Aignan") in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"), February 2013

Following the [2004 European parliament election](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2004 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), 37 [MEPs](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") from the UK, [Poland](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_Poland "2004 European Parliament election in Poland"), [Denmark](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_Denmark "2004 European Parliament election in Denmark") and [Sweden](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_Sweden "2004 European Parliament election in Sweden") founded a new European Parliamentary group called [Independence and Democracy](/wiki/Independence_and_Democracy "Independence and Democracy") as a direct successor to the EDD group.[[442]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhinnemoreMcGowan2013276Thorlakson201372-443) After the [2009 European parliament election](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), UKIP was a founder member of a new right-wing grouping called [Europe of Freedom and Democracy](/wiki/Europe_of_Freedom_and_Democracy "Europe of Freedom and Democracy") (EFD) comprising Eurosceptic, radical right, nationalist, national-conservative and other political factions.[[443]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynchWhitakerLoomes2012739Considère-Charon2010158Staab201167-444) This group was more right-wing than the previous term's Independence and Democracy group.[[444]](#cite_note-445)

Following the [2014 European parliament election](/wiki/2014_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2014 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), the EFD group was reconstituted as the [Europe of Freedom and Direct Democracy](/wiki/Europe_of_Freedom_and_Direct_Democracy "Europe of Freedom and Direct Democracy") (EFDD or EFD2) group on 24 June 2014, with a significant changes to group composition, including the [Five Star Movement](/wiki/Five_Star_Movement "Five Star Movement") of Italy, a total of 48 members.[[445]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015116–118-446)[[446]](#cite_note-447) The EFDD group lost official status in October 2014 when the defection of the Latvian MEP [Iveta Grigule](/wiki/Iveta_Grigule "Iveta Grigule") meant its membership no longer met the required number of states for Parliamentary groups (at least seven different member states).[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015118-448)[[448]](#cite_note-449) On 20 October, the EFDD announced it had restored the requisite seven state diversity by recruiting [Robert Iwaszkiewicz](/wiki/Robert_Iwaszkiewicz "Robert Iwaszkiewicz"), one of four representatives of the far-right Polish party [Congress of the New Right](/wiki/Congress_of_the_New_Right "Congress of the New Right").[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015118-448)[[449]](#cite_note-450) In December 2014 UKIP co-founded the [Alliance for Direct Democracy in Europe](/wiki/Alliance_for_Direct_Democracy_in_Europe "Alliance for Direct Democracy in Europe"), a [European political party](/wiki/European_political_party "European political party") whose membership is composed of several member parties of the EFDD parliamentary group.[[450]](#cite_note-451)

In the 2009–14 parliament, UKIP ranked 76th out of 76 for attendance, took part in 61% of votes, and had three of the six lowest attending MEPs,[[451]](#cite_note-452) which led to criticism from other parties and ex-UKIP MEPs that low participation may damage British interests.[[452]](#cite_note-times14-453) Between July 2014 and May 2015, its 23 MEPs maintained their record as the least active, participating on average in only 62.29% of votes.[[453]](#cite_note-454) In response to criticism of low participation by UKIP MEPs in the EU Parliament, Farage has said that "Our objective as MEPs is not to keep voting endlessly for more EU legislation and to take power away from Westminster."[[454]](#cite_note-455)

#### Members of the European Parliament

UKIP had no members in the European Parliament following the 2019 EU election. Twenty-four UKIP representatives were elected in the 2014 election, but twenty subsequently defected, one was expelled[[455]](#cite_note-456) and three lost their seats in the 2019 election.[[456]](#cite_note-457) For a full list of defections see [here](/wiki/List_of_British_politicians_who_have_crossed_the_floor#Members_of_the_European_Parliament_who_have_changed_party_affiliation "List of British politicians who have crossed the floor").

[James Carver](/wiki/James_Carver "James Carver") left UKIP to sit as an independent on 28 May 2018.[[457]](#cite_note-458) [William Dartmouth](/wiki/William_Legge,_10th_Earl_of_Dartmouth "William Legge, 10th Earl of Dartmouth") left the party on 26 September 2018 to sit as an independent, accusing Batten of "hijacking the party to campaign against Islam as a religion" and associating himself with "outlandish people and extreme right-wing groups".[[458]](#cite_note-459) [Bill Etheridge](/wiki/Bill_Etheridge "Bill Etheridge") followed shortly afterwards, on 2 October 2018, saying that the party under Batten's leadership "is seen by voters as a vehicle of hate towards Muslims and the gay community".[[459]](#cite_note-460)

In November 2018, [Patrick O'Flynn](/wiki/Patrick_O%27Flynn "Patrick O'Flynn") resigned to join the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(UK,_1990%E2%80%93present) "Social Democratic Party (UK, 1990–present)") in protest over UKIP's move to the "hard right",[[460]](#cite_note-461) and [Louise Bours](/wiki/Louise_Bours "Louise Bours") became an independent MEP.[[461]](#cite_note-462) Former leader Nigel Farage quit on 6 December 2018,[[462]](#cite_note-463) as did Scottish MEP David Coburn.[[463]](#cite_note-464) Another former leader, [Paul Nuttall](/wiki/Paul_Nuttall "Paul Nuttall"), quit the party the following day, as did [London Assembly](/wiki/London_Assembly "London Assembly") Member [Peter Whittle](/wiki/Peter_Whittle_(politician) "Peter Whittle (politician)").[[464]](#cite_note-465) It was reported that [Tim Aker](/wiki/Tim_Aker "Tim Aker") had also quietly quit the party earlier in 2018.[[465]](#cite_note-466) [Julia Reid](/wiki/Julia_Reid "Julia Reid") announced her resignation from UKIP on 8 December 2018,[[466]](#cite_note-467) with [Jonathan Bullock](/wiki/Jonathan_Bullock "Jonathan Bullock") following the next day.[[467]](#cite_note-468) [Jill Seymour](/wiki/Jill_Seymour "Jill Seymour"), [Jane Collins](/wiki/Jane_Collins "Jane Collins") and [Margot Parker](/wiki/Margot_Parker "Margot Parker") left for the Brexit Party on 15 April 2019, with the first of those three citing the party's current direction and occupation of 'the extreme right of politics'[[468]](#cite_note-469) and the second citing Batten's 'sick' defence of [Carl Benjamin's rape comments](/wiki/Carl_Benjamin#Rape_comments "Carl Benjamin").[[469]](#cite_note-470) On 17 April, Jonathan Arnott and Ray Finch both defected to The Brexit Party and, along with Seymour, Collins and Parker, sat in the EFDD group.[[470]](#cite_note-europarlEFDD-471)

As of April 2019, Batten and Agnew were members of the [Europe of Nations and Freedom](/wiki/Europe_of_Nations_and_Freedom "Europe of Nations and Freedom") group in the European Parliament[[471]](#cite_note-472) while Hookem was *[Non-Inscrit](/wiki/Non-Inscrit "Non-Inscrit")* (unattached). All lost their seats in the European Parliament in June 2019.

### General elections

During the 2010–15 Parliament, two Conservative MPs defected to UKIP and were re-elected in subsequent by-elections. At the 2015 general election, UKIP retained one of these seats ([Clacton](/wiki/Clacton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Clacton (UK Parliament constituency)")) and received over 30% of the vote in [Boston and Skegness](/wiki/Boston_and_Skegness_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Boston and Skegness (UK Parliament constituency)"), [South Thanet](/wiki/South_Thanet_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Thanet (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Heywood and Middleton](/wiki/Heywood_and_Middleton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Heywood and Middleton (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Thurrock](/wiki/Thurrock_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Thurrock (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Rochester and Strood](/wiki/Rochester_and_Strood_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Rochester and Strood (UK Parliament constituency)"). It lost its only seat in the 2017 election, when Clacton was regained by the Conservatives.

## Reception

### Other political groups

In campaigning on emotive issues, UKIP has proved divisive.[[479]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015xiii-480) Popular stereotypes have framed it as a far-right party,[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201592-370) and portrayed its activists as old white men holding offensive views.[[480]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015111-481) The party has faced vocal opposition from [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascist "Anti-fascist") groups such as [Hope not Hate](/wiki/Hope_not_Hate "Hope not Hate"), who have accused it of tapping into nationalist and xenophobic sentiment in its campaigns.[[481]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014147-482) Writing for *[The New York Times Magazine](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Magazine "The New York Times Magazine")*, [Geoffrey Wheatcroft](/wiki/Geoffrey_Wheatcroft "Geoffrey Wheatcroft") noted that there had been "a concerted campaign to brand UKIP as racist, an accusation that some of its own activists have done nothing to discourage."[[482]](#cite_note-483) Goodwin and [Caitlin Milazzo](/wiki/Caitlin_Milazzo "Caitlin Milazzo") highlighted that Farage had been "routinely ridiculed and dismissed", at best being portrayed as "a beer-swilling populist who wanted to drag Britain back to the 1950s" while at worst depicted as "a racist... would-be demagogue" who secretly wanted to overthrow the UK's liberal [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy").[[483]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo20153-484)

For many years, mainstream political figures derided or demeaned the importance of UKIP, although this did little to obstruct its electoral advances.[[484]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016172-485) By 2014, at which point UKIP was securing significant electoral support in the European Parliamentary elections, the main parties began to take it more seriously and devoted more time to countering the electoral threat it posed to them, in turn drawing more journalistic attention to the party.[[485]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016177-486) This increased attention gave the party the "oxygen of publicity" which helped bring the party to the attention of previously inattentive voters.[[486]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkeWhiteleyBorgesSanders2016144-487) Many on Britain's centre-left have been reluctant to accept that UKIP was hindering public support for Labour, instead believing that they were primarily a problem for the Conservatives and would thus help produce a Labour victory.[[487]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwin201515-488)[[488]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201562-489) Labour found that their campaign strategy of accusing UKIP of racism backfired, as rather than distancing UKIP supporters from the party it contributed to the perception that Labour failed to understand widespread concerns regarding immigration.[[489]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201543–44-490) A December 2014 poll by [ComRes](/wiki/ComRes "ComRes") found that voters saw UKIP as closer to the centre-ground of politics than the Conservatives.[[490]](#cite_note-491)

The [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") blamed UKIP for its decline, accusing the latter of stealing BNP policies and slogans.[[491]](#cite_note-492)

### Media and academia

The British press have publicised statements made by UKIP activists and candidates which have been regarded as racist, sexist or otherwise bigoted.[[492]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFordGoodwin2014282–83GoodwinMilazzo201546–47-493) Among the examples of UKIP representatives and supporters embarrassing the party have been an MEP who called for a ban on the construction of [mosques](/wiki/Mosques "Mosques") and for all British Muslims to sign a code of conduct, a councillor who suggested that shops should be allowed to refuse service to women and homosexuals, and a council candidate who compared Islam to [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") and told black comedian [Lenny Henry](/wiki/Lenny_Henry "Lenny Henry") to leave Britain after the latter called for greater ethnic diversity within the UK's creative industries.[[493]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201548-494) In 2015, a documentary called *Meet the Ukippers* filmed activists making racist statements; one said "the only people I do have a problem with are negroes".[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo2015231-272) For many years such individuals were internally tolerated within the party, although as part of Farage's push to professionalise the party a number of its members, such as MEP [Godfrey Bloom](/wiki/Godfrey_Bloom "Godfrey Bloom"), were expelled for making comments that brought UKIP into disrepute.[[494]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016173–74-495) In 2018, Jo Marney—who was then the girlfriend of the party leader Henry Bolton—was suspended from UKIP after it was revealed that she had sent texts stating that black Africans were "ugly". In these messages, she had criticised [Meghan Markle](/wiki/Meghan_Markle "Meghan Markle") for marrying into the [British royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family"), stating that Markle was "a dumb little commoner" and "a black American. Pushing their way to the top slowly. Next will be a Muslim PM and a black king."[[495]](#cite_note-496)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Nigel_Farage_talking_to_the_media.jpg/250px-Nigel_Farage_talking_to_the_media.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nigel_Farage_talking_to_the_media.jpg)

Farage talking to the media in 2012

In a May 2014 [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") survey, 47% considered the media to be biased against UKIP, which was double the percentage who deemed the media biased against any other party.[[496]](#cite_note-497)[[493]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodwinMilazzo201548-494) The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") received almost 1,200 complaints about its coverage of the 2014 European and local elections; 149 claimed that the BBC were biased against UKIP, while the rest claimed that it gave disproportionate attention to the party. The BBC defended its coverage.[[497]](#cite_note-498) Farage accused the BBC of a "liberal bias", particularly on issues of immigration, the EU, and climate change.[[498]](#cite_note-499)

David Deacon and Dominic Wring's examination of press coverage of UKIP during their 2014 campaign demonstrated that of the elite newspapers, the pro-EU titles *The Guardian* and *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer "The Observer")* gave the most coverage to perceived racist and intolerant aspects of the party, while the Eurosceptic titles *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* and *[The Sunday Times](/wiki/The_Sunday_Times "The Sunday Times")* instead focused on questioning the propriety and integrity of UKIP representatives.[[499]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016180-500) Among the populist tabloids, *[The Sun](/wiki/The_Sun_(United_Kingdom) "The Sun (United Kingdom)")*/*[Sun on Sunday](/wiki/Sun_on_Sunday "Sun on Sunday")* and the *[Daily Mirror](/wiki/Daily_Mirror "Daily Mirror")*/*[Sunday Mirror](/wiki/Sunday_Mirror "Sunday Mirror")* were found to contain the most negative coverage of UKIP, while the *[Daily Express](/wiki/Daily_Express "Daily Express")* and *[Sunday Express](/wiki/Sunday_Express "Sunday Express")*—owned by UKIP donor Richard Desmond—gave significantly lower coverage to the gaffes and prejudices of UKIP representatives.[[499]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016180-500) Deacon and Wring noted that the majority of those right-wing newspapers that share UKIP's views on immigration also share the perspective of more liberal newspapers that many of UKIP's interventions are racist.[[500]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016182-501) This right-wing press opposition to UKIP may result from the allegiance that these newspapers have to the Conservatives, and resulting perception of UKIP as an electoral threat.[[500]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeaconWring2016182-501)

Academic research has been carried out into UKIP. In 2016, it was noted that most of this had focused on examining the party's electoral support base, its consequences for other parties, and the possibilities and prospects of a referendum on continued EU membership, with little having focused on an examination of the party's policies.[[501]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReed2016226-502) Two currents have emerged among those seeking to interpret UKIP: the first, and generally older, current views them as a manifestation of Britain's strong Eurosceptic movement, while the second seeks to explain their position in the British parliamentary system while drawing upon the comparative literature on right-wing populist parties elsewhere in Europe.[[502]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDye20153–4-503)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-legalleader_385-0)** Diane James won the [September 2016 leadership election](/wiki/September_2016_UK_Independence_Party_leadership_election "September 2016 UK Independence Party leadership election") but resigned 18 days later, prior to taking office. As the relevant paperwork required by the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)") was not processed before her resignation due to her writing *[vi coactus](/wiki/Vi_coactus "Vi coactus")* after her signature on the paperwork,[[383]](#cite_note-383) legally Farage remained the leader of UKIP during James's tenure.[[384]](#cite_note-384) Farage continued to act as interim leader of UKIP until the [November 2016](/wiki/November_2016_UK_Independence_Party_leadership_election "November 2016 UK Independence Party leadership election") election.
